title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Claire Evans, who works as a paramedic.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today I'm interviewing Claire Evans, who works as a paramedic. Claire, what made you choose this career? || Guest: I first wanted to be a nurse, and I did some work experience in a hospital. But [20]I realised I liked helping people at the moment an emergency happens, before they reach hospital[/20]. That's why paramedic work appealed to me. || Interviewer: What was the hardest part of training? || Guest: The medical information was a lot to learn, but I expected that. [21]What I found hardest was staying calm while several people were asking me questions at once[/21]. Real situations can feel noisy and confusing. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy about working in an ambulance team? || Guest: You never work completely alone. [22]I like the trust between two crew members, because each person knows what the other is doing without needing long explanations[/22]. That makes the work safer. || Interviewer: Do people ever call an ambulance when they don't really need one? || Guest: Sometimes, but you can't judge too quickly. [23]A problem that sounds small on the phone can turn out to be serious when you arrive[/23]. So we treat every call carefully. || Interviewer: How do you relax after a difficult shift? || Guest: I used to go straight to bed, but my mind kept working. [24]Now I usually walk my dog for half an hour, which helps me separate work from home[/24]. It sounds simple, but it works. || Interviewer: What advice would you give someone interested in this job? || Guest: Learn first aid, of course, but also practise listening. [25]Patients are often frightened, so being able to explain things in a clear, kind way is just as important as medical knowledge[/25]. || Interviewer: Are there any moments that stay with you in a positive way? || Guest: Yes, many. People remember big rescues, but small things matter too. An elderly man once wrote to say that I had made him feel less afraid on the way to hospital. I kept that letter because it reminded me why calm words matter. || Interviewer: Has the job changed how you see everyday life? || Guest: Definitely. I don't worry so much about little problems now. If a train is late or a shop is closed, I can usually keep things in perspective. The job shows you how quickly life can change, so I try to enjoy ordinary days more. || Interviewer: What do you wish people knew before an ambulance arrives? || Guest: Simple information helps a lot. If someone can tell us what happened, what medicines the patient takes and whether they have allergies, we can work faster. Even clearing space near the patient can make a difference. || Interviewer: Do you work the same area every day? || Guest: Mostly, but not always. Working locally means I know many roads and care homes, which helps. Sometimes we cover another area when it is busy, and then teamwork and clear directions become even more important. || Interviewer: Is there a busy time of year? || Guest: Winter is often busy because older people can fall, and breathing problems are more common. Summer has different problems, especially at outdoor events. Every season brings something new, so you have to stay ready and flexible.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today I'm interviewing Claire Evans, who works as a paramedic. Claire, what made you choose this career?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I first wanted to be a nurse, and I did some work experience in a hospital. But I realised I liked helping people at the moment an emergency happens, before they reach hospital. That's why paramedic work appealed to me.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was the hardest part of training?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The medical information was a lot to learn, but I expected that. What I found hardest was staying calm while several people were asking me questions at once. Real situations can feel noisy and confusing.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy about working in an ambulance team?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: You never work completely alone. I like the trust between two crew members, because each person knows what the other is doing without needing long explanations. That makes the work safer.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do people ever call an ambulance when they don't really need one?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Sometimes, but you can't judge too quickly. A problem that sounds small on the phone can turn out to be serious when you arrive. So we treat every call carefully.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you relax after a difficult shift?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I used to go straight to bed, but my mind kept working. Now I usually walk my dog for half an hour, which helps me separate work from home. It sounds simple, but it works.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give someone interested in this job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Learn first aid, of course, but also practise listening. Patients are often frightened, so being able to explain things in a clear, kind way is just as important as medical knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Are there any moments that stay with you in a positive way?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, many. People remember big rescues, but small things matter too. An elderly man once wrote to say that I had made him feel less afraid on the way to hospital. I kept that letter because it reminded me why calm words matter.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has the job changed how you see everyday life?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Definitely. I don't worry so much about little problems now. If a train is late or a shop is closed, I can usually keep things in perspective. The job shows you how quickly life can change, so I try to enjoy ordinary days more.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you wish people knew before an ambulance arrives?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Simple information helps a lot. If someone can tell us what happened, what medicines the patient takes and whether they have allergies, we can work faster. Even clearing space near the patient can make a difference.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you work the same area every day?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Mostly, but not always. Working locally means I know many roads and care homes, which helps. Sometimes we cover another area when it is busy, and then teamwork and clear directions become even more important.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is there a busy time of year?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Winter is often busy because older people can fall, and breathing problems are more common. Summer has different problems, especially at outdoor events. Every season brings something new, so you have to stay ready and flexible.

questions.question: Why did Claire decide to become a paramedic?

options.A: She wanted to help people before they reached hospital.
options.B: She could not get a place on a nursing course.
options.C: She preferred driving to working indoors.

questions.explanation: Claire says she liked helping people when an emergency happens, before they reach hospital.

questions.question: What did Claire find hardest during training?
questions.explanation: She says the hardest thing was staying calm while several people asked questions at once.

questions.question: What does Claire like about ambulance teamwork?

options.A: She always works with the same person.
options.B: She can ask her partner to make decisions.
options.C: Crew members understand each other quickly.

questions.explanation: She likes the trust between crew members and how they know what each other is doing.

questions.question: What does Claire say about emergency calls?

options.A: A call may be more serious than it first seems.
options.B: Too many people call for small problems.
options.C: Most calls are easy to understand by phone.

questions.explanation: She says a problem that sounds small on the phone can turn out to be serious.

questions.question: How does Claire relax after work now?

options.B: She takes her dog for a walk.

questions.explanation: She says she usually walks her dog for half an hour after a difficult shift.

questions.question: What skill does Claire say future paramedics need?

options.B: The ability to write reports quickly.
options.C: The ability to explain things kindly and clearly.

questions.explanation: She says explaining things in a clear, kind way is as important as medical knowledge.
